item.title: U.S. Targets Russian Signal/WhatsApp Hackers as WhatsApp Adds Usernames, Mustang Panda Hits India
item.content:encoded:
Mustang Panda Uses Zoho WorkDrive as Command Channel in Indian Government Attacks — Must Read

Mustang Panda is targeting Indian government and hydropower organizations with spear-phishing campaigns that deploy new malware and abuse Zoho WorkDrive as a covert command-and-control and data exfiltration channel. Defenders should watch for signed-binary DLL sideloading, suspicious Zoho API use from non-browser processes, and related indicators such as the RunOnece persistence typo and couldinstallup[.]com.

  • Acronis Threat Research Unit found active compromises inside Indian government networks, including machines used by senior administrative staff, and worked with CERT-In on notification and cleanup.
  • Mustang Panda used Zoho WorkDrive as a command channel by reading commands from an inbox folder and writing stolen output to an outbox in an attacker-controlled account.
  • The campaigns used ZIP archives with hidden malicious DLLs and lures themed around a hydropower cooperation proposal and a memorandum of understanding between Indian and Taiwanese institutions.
  • Acronis identified three tools in the campaigns: SHARDLOADER, MINIRECON, and ZOHOMURK.
  • Acronis assessed that the goal was intelligence on India’s hydropower plans and defense ties with Taiwan, and attributed the activity to Mustang Panda with high confidence.

U.S. offers $10 million for hackers targeting WhatsApp, Signal users — Should Read

The U.S. State Department is offering up to $10 million for information on Russian-linked UNC5792 and UNC4221 hackers targeting Signal and WhatsApp accounts of U.S., NATO, and Ukraine-related officials and researchers. The groups use phishing and impersonation to steal Signal backup recovery keys and access victims’ past communications, though the messaging platforms’ encryption has not been compromised.

  • The U.S. Department of State is offering up to $10 million for information that helps identify or locate members of the UNC5792 and UNC4221 hacker groups, which are linked to Russia's intelligence and military services.
  • The bounty is part of the Rewards for Justice program, which targets foreign state actors carrying out cyberattacks against U.S. critical infrastructure.
  • UNC5792 has conducted phishing campaigns targeting Signal and WhatsApp accounts of U.S. government officials, military leadership, and allied personnel.
  • The FBI and CISA updated a March 2026 advisory with new tactics attributed to UNC5792 and UNC4221, including stealing Signal Backup Recovery Keys.
  • U.S. authorities said the communication platforms and their encryption have not been compromised, but thousands of individual accounts for commercial messaging applications were compromised through these attacks.

Microsoft extends Windows Server 2022 hotpatching until October 2027 — May Read

Microsoft is extending hotpatch support for Windows Server 2022 Datacenter: Azure Edition through October 2027, letting enrolled systems keep receiving monthly security updates without restarts for an extra year. Regular non-hotpatch updates, such as some non-security and .NET updates, may still require reboots.

  • Microsoft has extended Windows Server 2022 hotpatching until October 2027, one year after the mainstream end date of October 2026.
  • The extension applies only to systems running Windows Server 2022 Datacenter: Azure Edition that are enrolled in hotpatch updates.
  • Hotpatching deploys security updates by patching the in-memory code of running processes without restarting them after each installation or rebooting the device.
  • Servers still require a restart after installing updates delivered through the regular Windows update channel, including Windows non-security updates and non-Windows updates such as .NET patches.
  • The Datacenter, Datacenter: Azure Edition, Essentials, and Standard editions of Windows Server 2022 will reach the extended end date on October 14, 2031.

US racks up about 400 wins over illegal World Cup streaming sites — May Read

The U.S. government seized nearly 400 domains illegally streaming World Cup matches, with help from FIFA, NBC Universal and international partners. Officials said the sites violated copyright law and also exposed viewers to risks like malware and insecure connections.

  • The U.S. Department of Justice announced the seizure of nearly 400 internet domains that were illegally streaming World Cup matches.
  • Homeland Security Investigations said unauthorized streaming sites violate copyright laws and can expose viewers to malware attacks and insecure connections that may compromise personal and financial data.
  • FIFA helped identify the domains, along with U.S. broadcaster NBC Universal and other entities.
  • Servers and domains linked to illegal World Cup streaming were targeted in Peru and Bulgaria, with additional disruptions in Croatia, Romania, Poland and Colombia.
  • Homeland Security Investigations led a similar operation during the 2022 World Cup that seized more than 70 sites.

WhatsApp is Finally Getting Usernames to Help Keep Phone Numbers Private — May Read

WhatsApp is rolling out optional username reservations globally so users can connect without exposing their phone numbers. The feature includes unique usernames and optional username keys to limit who can contact a user for the first time.

  • WhatsApp announced the start of global username reservations to help protect the privacy of more than three billion users on the platform.
  • The optional feature will let users connect through usernames instead of directly sharing phone numbers, with general availability planned for later this year.
  • WhatsApp said there will be no directory to browse and no suggestions, so someone must know the exact username to contact a user for the first time.
  • Users can set up a username key as an extra layer of protection, requiring first-time contacts to know both the exact username and the key.
  • Content creators, small businesses, and organizations can choose to claim their existing Instagram or Facebook username on WhatsApp.

⚡ Weekly Recap: Linux Kernel Flaws, AI Malware Tricks, Turla Backdoor, Infostealers and More — Optional

This week’s key takeaway is that defenders need to prioritize patching and hardening common systems, with actively exploited flaws like DirtyClone in Linux and PTC Windchill/FlexPLM RCE creating immediate risk. The recap also highlights growing AI-related abuse, from malware that tricks AI analysis tools to phishing and coding-agent prompt injection attacks.

  • Researchers detailed DirtyClone, a Linux kernel flaw tracked as CVE-2026-43503 that can let local users gain root privileges via cloned packets on Debian, Ubuntu, and Fedora systems with default namespace configurations.
  • A critical remote code execution vulnerability in PTC Windchill PDMlink and PTC FlexPLM, tracked as CVE-2026-12569, has been actively exploited to deploy JSP web shells on vulnerable systems.
  • A newly discovered macOS malware called Gaslight uses embedded prompt injection strings and fake debugging data to confuse AI-assisted malware analysis tools and has been attributed with high confidence to a North Korean-linked threat actor.
  • Turla has used a previously undocumented .NET backdoor called STOCKSTAY in attacks targeting government and military organizations in Ukraine and entities interested in Italian foreign policy.
  • Operation Endgame disrupted infrastructure tied to Amadey and StealC, including 326 servers and 142 domains, and recovered about 27 million credentials stolen from over 385,000 compromised systems.

29th June – Threat Intelligence Report — Optional

This week’s threat intelligence roundup highlights major supply-chain, telecom, government, and cloud breaches alongside rapidly growing AI-enabled phishing and agent abuse. Organizations should prioritize patching actively exploited flaws in Cisco, Ubiquiti, Dify, and Langflow while tightening controls around cloud credentials, third-party code, and AI tools.

  • Polymarket confirmed a supply chain attack in which malicious JavaScript was injected into its website through a breached third-party frontend vendor, leading to about $3 million stolen from fewer than 15 accounts.
  • KDDI reported an intrusion into its ISP email platform that may have compromised up to 14.22 million email addresses and passwords across services from six Japanese ISPs.
  • Researchers detailed EvilTokens, an AI-powered phishing-as-a-service operation that abuses device-code authentication to steal Microsoft 365 tokens.
  • Cisco addressed CVE-2026-20245, a high-severity command injection flaw in Catalyst SD-WAN Manager that attackers exploited as a zero-day for months.
  • Researchers uncovered the FortiBleed campaign, which turned compromised FortiGate firewalls into passive credential stealers and siphoned more than 110 million credentials.

Agentic AI Has an Identity Problem and Attackers Know It — Optional

Agentic AI is creating a new identity security problem because autonomous agents often use poorly inventoried, overprivileged credentials to act across enterprise systems. Organizations should govern every AI agent with distinct identities, ownership, scoped and time-bound access, visibility, logging, and revocation controls.

  • Agentic AI agents can authenticate, receive permissions, call APIs, write code, trigger workflows, query databases, and take action across production environments.
  • Many organizations already have AI agents using credentials, API tokens, OAuth grants, and cloud roles that have not been fully inventoried.
  • Traditional identity and access programs fall short because AI agents can interpret goals, choose paths, act across systems, and scale at machine speed.
  • The article identifies visibility gaps, overprivileged access, and prompt injection or indirect manipulation as three critical problems for agentic AI security.
  • The article argues that every AI agent should have a distinct identity, an owner, a business purpose, an approved scope of action, and a defined lifecycle.

item.title: U.S. Bounties Russian Messaging Hackers as Mustang Panda and SimpleHelp Exploits Surface
Microsoft Purges StegoAd Edge Extensions as libssh2 CVE-2026-55200 PoC Emerges
KDDI Email System Breach Exposes Up to 14.2 Million ISP Logins
Gmail Legal-Notice Phishing Uses short.gy to Hide Credential-Harvest Domain
KDDI Email Breach Exposes 14.2 Million Logins Amid Phishing Campaign Using Gmail Shortlinks for Credential Harvesting
Russian SMS Phishing Targets Messaging Accounts; Clean GitHub Repos Abuse Claude Code
